pages.text: Although all having a similar setting time, the cement pastes with different M/P ratios in fact possess distinguishable physical and chemical properties. The strength developments of the four MKPC pastes during the ﬁrst month after casting are compared in Fig. 3 . It shows that at all ages, the higher the molar ratio of M/P, the higher the compressive strength of the cement paste. For the paste with an M/P ratio of 10, it has the highest mechanical properties among the different pastes. In addition, it has the most rapid strength development with a 1-day strength already reach- ing over 70% of the 28-day’s value. While for the paste with an M/P ratio of 2, its compressive strength is the lowest and only as high as about half of that of M/P ratio of 10 at the age of 28-day. A higher physical strength could mean a more compacted system, healthier crystal growth, better formulation, and higher efﬁciency of the constituents consumption. The chemical reactions of the MgO-KH 2PO4-H2O ternary system are kicked off by the dissociation of KDP when it comes into contact with water during the mixing stage. The resultant acidic environment of the mixture would induce the dissociation of magnesia followed by the crystallization of the principal reaction product, magnesium potassium phosphate hexahydrate (MKP), MgKPO4/C1 6H2O. The chemical reaction of the hexahydrate forma- tion is given in Eq. (1) accompanied with the corresponding stan- dard enthalpy change of formation DHf of the reactants [33,34]. MgO þ KH2PO4 þ 5H2O ! MgKPO4 /C1 6H2O ð1Þ DHf /C0 602 /C0 1568 /C0 286 /C0 3724 ðkJ=molÞ At room temperature, the standard enthalpy change of reaction DHr of the Eq. (1) can be calculated as /C0 124 kJ/mol, which suggest- ing an exothermic reaction. The above equation also implies that to produce 1 mol hexahydrate, it needs 1 mol magnesium oxide and 1 mol potassium phosphate. Therefore, the theoretical molar ratio of magnesium to phosphate required in the starting materials for the exothermic reaction is a unity, provided that all of the reactants are completely consumed. In the reality, however, the raw materi- als could not be totally consumed during the rapid reaction espe- cially for MgO which is acquired by dissolution of the magnesia in the highly concentrated heterogeneous system. In view of the mechanical strength measurements of the four cements pastes, an M/P ratio of 10 could be the best for MKPC paste formulation. Also even by considering a 100% consumption of KDP, the corre- sponding usage of magnesia in the production of the hexahydrates can be calculated at most only 10%. In the other words, with a lower M/P ratio, both magnesia and phosphate could not be fully utilized. Therefore, it is expected that phosphate residuals could be readily found in the paste with a lower M/P ratio, while much more magnesia residuals in the paste with a higher M/P ratio. The above analysis can be veriﬁed by determining the crystalline phase compositions of the cement pastes along with the raw mate- rials by using X-ray diffraction technique. The X-ray diffractograms of the raw materials and the cement pastes with lower M/P ratios are stacked in Fig. 4 . It shows that the locations of the strongest characteristic peaks of MgO (Fig. 4 a), KDP ( Fig. 4 b), and MKP hexahydrate ( Fig. 4 c) are located wide apart at about 43, 24, and 21 /C1762-theta, respectively. The stron- gest peaks of the raw materials actually extends several times the height of the ﬁgure, in

The microstructural observations of the different crystalline phases in the MKPC system are veriﬁed by compositional analysis which agree well with the theoretical calculations. It is believed that the current study provides sound information for further development of the ternary cementing system for rapid repair. Microstructure of magnesium potassium phosphate cement C.K. Chau, Fei Qiao, Zongjin Li ⇑ Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Hong Kong article info Article history: Received 17 September 2010 Received in revised form 18 October 2010 Accepted 20 December 2010 Available online 3 February 2011 Keywords: Acid–base reaction Cement Magnesium potassium phosphate Microstructure abstract The ever growing concerns over global warming and other detrimental ecological changes have spurred the research and development of new types of cementitious materials. Along this line, magnesium potas- sium phosphate cement (MKPC) appears as one of the most promising candidates, especially in the ﬁeld of rapid repair. A systematic study of the microstructure of MKPC is conducted, starting from paste design and phase identiﬁcation, to morphology examination and compositional analysis. The experimental results show that the mechanical properties of the cement pastes are inﬂuenced by the crystallinity and growth of magnesium potassium phosphate hexahydrate, the principal reaction product of the heterogeneous system. The ﬁnal form of the hexahydrate developed in the matrix largely depends on the growth conditions, which is closely related to the calculated molar ratio of magnesia to phosphate (M/P). It is demonstrated that a poor crystal growth and a signiﬁcant amount of unreacted raw materials can be observed in the cement pastes with low M/P ratios, while a better crystal growth associated with high early strength can be achieved for the pastes with an M/P ratio as high as ten folds of the theoretical one. Finally, the microstructural observations of the different crystalline phases in MKPC are further ver- iﬁed by compositional analysis which agree well with the theoretical values. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction With an annual global production reaching 2.8 billion metric tons [1], ordinary Portland cement is kept as the most versatile construction material since the utilization of ﬂint by human beings in the Paleolithic times. The production of 1000 kg of the hydraulic cement, however, would generate 900 kg of carbon dioxide and consume 5 billion joules of electrical power and fuel energy. The ever growing concerns over global warming and other detrimental ecological changes has spurred the research and development of new kinds of cementitious materials produced from other re- sources. It is believed that magnesium phosphate cement [2,3] is one of the most promising candidates that could partially replace ordinary cement, especially in the ﬁeld of rapid repair [4–6]. Mag- nesium phosphate cement is a chemically bonded [7,8] cementi- tious binder in which the phosphate bonding [9] is formed via heterogeneous through-solution [10] reactions between dead burnt magnesia and phosphate [11]. Dead burnt magnesia is pro- duced by calcination of magnesite, which is a very high production mineral with a worldwide annual production over 14 million tons [12]. The development of cementing materials with phosphate bonding was originated by the need of a quick setting ﬁller for dental restorations [13]. During the early stages of the studies of phosphate cement [14], products from various types of basic metal oxides associated with different phosphate solutions were evalu- ated for a wide ranges of applications [15,16]. By the deﬁnitions of electron pair acceptor and donator [17],

phosphate hexahydrate, or K-type struvite, was identiﬁed as an- other naturally existed phosphate mineral [31]. Recent studies reveal that the properties of the MgO-KDP-H 2O cementing system are mainly affected by the reactivity of magne- sia, molar ratio of magnesium to phosphate (M/P), as well as water usage [6,32]. To further understand the newly developed magne- sium potassium phosphate cement (MKPC) system, it is highly desirable to study the microstructure of the different crystalline phases in the matrix, which can strongly inﬂuence the physical and chemical properties of the cement, and in turn govern its industrial applications. By virtue of the modern microscopy tech- nology, a systematic study of the MKPC microstructures, starting from rational paste formulation and phase identiﬁcation, to mor- phology examination and compositional analysis, has been con- ducted. The crystal habits of magnesium potassium phosphate hexahydrate developed under different crystallization conditions were examined. It was demonstrated that a poor crystal growth and a signiﬁcant amount of unreacted raw materials would be found in the MKPC pastes with low M/P ratios, while a better crys- tal growth associated with high early strength can be achieved for the pastes with high M/P ratios. 2. Experimental program The starting materials used for casting magnesium potassium phosphate ce- ment (MKPC) pastes are dead burnt magnesia, soluble potassium dihydrogen phos- phate (KH 2PO4), borax (Na 2B4O7/C1 10H2O) and potable water. With a magnesium oxide (MgO) content of 84% by weight, the magnesia was obtained from Jinan, Shandong Province, China. The chemical composition of the raw material was determined by X-ray ﬂuorescence spectrometer (XRF, JEOL, JSX-3201Z), as shown in Table 1 . In addition, the particle size distribution of the magnesia powder was measured by laser particle size analyzer (Coulter, LS230). Chemical grade potassium phosphate and borax, both in powder form with a purity of higher than 99%, were adopted in the MKPC paste formulation. To study the microstructure and morphol- ogy of different possible reaction products, paste specimens with a wide range of molar ratios of magnesium to phosphate (M/P) were prepared as set forth in Table 2. The fresh cement pastes were produced by mixing magnesia, KDP, borax and potable water for about 3 min, and then poured into prism moulds of 40 /C2 40 /C2 160 mm 3 with vibration compaction. The setting time of the cement pastes was determined by using Vicat needle (ELE) according to ASTM standard C191. The specimens were demoulded within 24 h followed by air curing at a temperature of 22 ± 1 /C176C and under a relative humidity of 50 ± 5%. Strength development of the specimens at the ages of 1-day, 3-day, 7-day, and 28-day was assessed by using an automatic concrete compression machine (Unit Test, ADS2000) according to ASTM standard C109. The crystalline phases of the cement pastes were identiﬁed by a powder X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, X’pert Pro) which is equipped with a graphite monochromator in the diffracted beam. The diffraction data were collected in the 2-theta angle range from 10 /C176to 45/C176using Cu K a radiation (1.541874 Å, 40 mA, 40 kV) with a step size of 0.05 /C176. The powder samples for diffraction measurements were gathered by crushing the prism specimens and passing the ﬁne particles through a sieve with a screen aper- ture of 75 lm (MTS-157). Finally, the morphology and microstru

materialswithverydifferentmorphologies. Locatingat therighthand sideofthemicrograph,theﬁrstkindisamassivecompactparticlewitha ﬂat and smooth surface. While the other kind is located at the left hand side, and has an asbestiform texture consisting of numerous acicular polycrystals.Thecentralportionofthemicrographunderahighermag- niﬁcation is shown in Fig. 7, revealing more details on the geometry of the slender crystals. The size of those acicular crystals running the full diagonal of the SEM photo can be estimated as about 0.5lm wide and over 15 lm long. The identities of the two kinds of materials could be characterizedbyutilizingenergydispersiveX-ray(EDS)spectroscopy. Given in Fig. 8 are the energy spectrums and elemental statis- tics from the scanning on the selected window areas in Fig. 6 . The major elements detected are magnesium (Mg), potassium (K), phosphorous (P), and oxygen (O), which are all key elements of the MgO–KH 2PO4–H2O system under study. For the scanned area 1 ( Fig. 8 a), the molar ratio of Mg:K:P is 0.01:1:1.07, which means that the amount of Mg is almost zero and the amount of K equaling P. The measured molar ratio agrees well with the theo- retical molar ratio among the elements in KDP (KH 2PO4). Hence, the bulk particle with a ﬂat and smooth surface as shown in Fig. 6 can be identiﬁed as KDP residual. For the scanned area 2 (Fig. 8 b), the molar ratio of Mg:K:P:O is 0.51:1:1.01, which means that the Mg content is largely increased in this area, while the contents of K and P are almost the same. To review, the theoretical value of the molar ratio among Mg, K, and P for the reaction product MKP is 1:1:1 (see Eq. (1)). For the acicular crystals under scanning, the relative amount of Mg is halved, or more appropri- ately, the relative amount of K and P are doubled, most probably due to the contamination from the large quantity of KDP residuals nearby. Nevertheless, the slender polycrystals with an aspect ratio as large as 30 can be identiﬁed as the MKP products. With an in- crease of the M/P ratio, the amount of KDP residual would be re- duced and the molar ratio among the detected elements Mg, K, and P is expected to match the theoretical one. Under better reac- tion conditions with reasonable relative concentrations of the reac- tants, the crystal growth should be more healthy leading to a bigger size of the resultant polycrystals. That can be veriﬁed by the microstructural observation of ce- ment paste #2 with an M/P ratio of 5, as shown in Fig. 9 . By com- paring with the needle-like polycrystals shown in Fig. 7 under the same magniﬁcation, the reaction products of the paste #2 are much bigger in size. The crystal habit is changed from acicular to bladed prismatic, which is demonstrated clearly by the two central crystals resting against each other. The cross sectional area of the bigger blade lying under can be estimated as about 1.5 /C2 7.5 lm, or 11 lm2. While the cross sectional areas of the polycrystals, which might be broken during sample preparation, are ﬂat and smooth, the lateral surfaces are irregular and wrinkled. It can be seen that the other crystals with varied sizes around also have more or less the same crystal habit. An EDS line scan was per- formed on the cross section of the upper one of the two adjacent polycrystals, and the analysis is given next to the SEM photo in Fig. 9 . It shows that the elemental compositions of the polycrystal is quite homogeneous across its width of 5 lm. The measure

of a unity. That could be attributed to the high calculated M/P ratio used in the formulation of the cement paste. Therefore, a large amount of magnesia residuals is expected, which are near by, underneath, and most of the cases embedded in the reaction prod- ucts. As a result, the polycrystals with subhedral surfaces under examination would be most probably contaminated with magnesia leading to a higher relative Mg contents detected by EDS scan. 4. Conclusions A systematic study of the microstructure of magnesium potas- sium phosphate cement (MKPC) is presented, starting from rational paste formulation and phase identiﬁcation to microstruc- ture observation and compositional analysis. With a wide range of calculated magnesium to phosphate (M/P) molar ratios, the MgO-KH2PO4-H2O system exhibits more or less similar brief set- ting period, but signiﬁcant differences in physical and chemical properties. The experimental results show that the mechanical strength of MKPC paste may be dictated by the rational formula- tion of the starting materials, in turn the crystallinity and growth of the main reaction product of the ternary heterogeneous system. The major crystalline phases observed in MKPC are magnesium potassium phosphate hexahydrate, as well as the residuals of raw materials, including magnesia and potassium phosphate. On the one hand, with a very big size and a ﬂat surface, the raw materials residuals can be easily identiﬁed by their appearances. On the other hand, as an isostructure of struvite, the hexahydrate poly- crystals generally elongate along the crystallographic a-axis. The ﬁ- nal form of the hexahydrate developed in the cement pastes largely depends on the growth conditions, which is closely related to the M/P ratio adopted. With an M/P ratio as low as mere 2, the hexa- hydrate would grow into slender acicular polycrystals with large aspect ratio. As the M/P ratio increases, the hexahydrate could grow much larger in size and turn into bladed and prismatic habits with wrinkled surface. To achieve the most rapid strength develop- ment and higher efﬁciency in materials consumption, an M/P ratio as high as 10 should be used. As a result, a more compacted micro- structure will be formed, in which the polycrystals of the reaction products covered by a subhedral surface might be difﬁcult to be recognized. Area 3 Fig. 10. A large size dull particle surrounded by the reaction products. Fig. 11. A prismatic polycrystal covered with subhedral surfaces.
